title: Spin Hall effect in electronic Lévy glasses: Enhanced spin current generation in the superdiffusive regime
authors: Diego B. Fonseca, Luiz Felipe C. Pereira, Anderson L. R. Barbosa
abstract: In spintronics, both electronic charge and spin are used to process and store information. Generation, manipulation, and detection of spin currents are essential for the development of next-generation spintronic technologies. Here, we investigate the spin Hall effect in electronic Lévy glasses composed of graphene ribbons with randomly distributed circular regions of high spin-orbit coupling. These systems exhibit two transport regimes that can be tuned by adjusting the Fermi energy. The superdiffusive regime is characterized by low Fermi energy, low resistivity, and low magnetoresistivity, resulting in a long spin diffusion length, in contrast to the diffusive regime. Employing the Landauer-Büttiker approach in conjunction with numerically exact tight-binding simulations, we compute spin-resolved transmission coefficients to assess the spin Hall current and the spin Hall angle as functions of Fermi energy, spin-orbit coupling strength, and on-site electrostatic potential. Our findings reveal that, in the superdiffusive regime, a low charge current can be converted into a large spin Hall current, whereas in the diffusive regime, the same charge current generates a modest spin Hall current. Moreover, we observe that the spin Hall angle can reach 30% in the superdiffusive regime, whereas in the diffusive regime it is only 5%. These results demonstrate that electronic Lévy glasses provide a versatile platform for controlling spin transport and optimizing the spin Hall effect for spintronic applications.
summary: This paper demonstrates that electronic Levy glasses can significantly enhance the Spin Hall Effect by utilizing a superdiffusive transport regime. By engineering the disorder geometry in graphene nanoribbons, researchers can achieve much higher charge-to-spin conversion efficiency compared to standard diffusive systems.
main_problem: The study investigates how the transition from diffusive to superdiffusive transport regimes in electronic Levy glasses affects the efficiency of the Spin Hall Effect (SHE).
main_result: In the superdiffusive regime, the Spin Hall Angle (SHA) can reach up to 30-35%, significantly higher than the 5% observed in the diffusive regime, and can be further tuned via electrostatic potential.
method: Numerical simulations using the Landauer-Büttiker approach and the Kwant software package, employing a tight-binding model with ensemble averaging over 10^3 configurations.
model_or_system: Graphene nanoribbons (Armchair and Zigzag) acting as electronic Levy glasses, featuring randomly distributed circular regions of high Bychkov-Rashba spin-orbit coupling with a power-law distribution of radii.
key_observables: Longitudinal charge current (Ic), transverse spin Hall current (Isz), Spin Hall Angle (SHA), and Spin-polarized density of states (SDOS).
important_parameters: Fermi energy (E), spin-orbit coupling strength (lambda), on-site electrostatic potential (U), and the power-law exponent (beta).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using a tight-binding approximation with nearest-neighbor hopping and localized spin-orbit coupling clusters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the device geometry and SOC clusters; Figure 2 shows transport properties as functions of Fermi energy; Figure 4 compares spin density of states in different regimes; Figure 5 demonstrates the effect of electrostatic potential on spin current and SHA.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem of spin-to-charge conversion, describes the electronic Levy glass model and tight-binding Hamiltonian, presents numerical results comparing diffusive and superdiffusive regimes, and concludes with the implications for disorder engineering in spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of (up) AGNR and (down) ZGNR (black sites) connected to four semi-infinite leads (red sites). Cir- cular regions represent the graphene proximity-coupled to a high-SOC material. A charge current (longitudinal arrow) gives rise to spin Hall current (transversal arrows). Vi is the potential in each lead.

FIG. 2. Averages of (a,b) longitudinal charge current, (c,d) transverse spin Hall current, and (e,f) spin Hall angle as functions of the Fermi energy. The top row (a, c, e) corresponds to AGNR results, while the bottom row (b, d, f) corresponds to ZGNR. Different symbols indicate different SOC strength: λ = 0.04t (black circles), λ = 0.1t (red squares), λ = 0.3t (green diamonds), and λ = 0.8t (blue triangles). Lines are guides to the eye. The averages are computed over 103 different samples.

FIG. 3. Averages of (a,b) longitudinal charge current, (c,d) transverse spin Hall current, and (e,f) spin Hall angle as functions of the Bychkov-Rashba spin-orbit coupling strength λ. The top row (a, c, e) corresponds to AGNR results, while the bottom row (b, d, f) corresponds to ZGNR. Different symbols indicate different regimes: superdiffusive E = 0.07t (black circles), diffusive E = 0.45t (red squares). Lines are guides to the eye. Each data point represents an average computed over 103 independent samples.

FIG. 4. SDOS ⟨Sz⟩for AGNR with λ = 0.04t, 0.1t, 0.3t and 0.8t, top to bottom respectively. Left hand side column refers to a system in the superdiffusive regime E = 0.07t, while the right hand side corresponds to the diffusive one E = 0.45t.

FIG. 5. Average (a,b) transversal spin current and (c,d) SHA as functions of the on-site potential U at fixed SOC strength λ = 0.2t. The top row shows AGNR results, while the bot- tom row shows ZGNR. Different symbols indicate different regimes: superdiffusive E = 0.07t (black circles), diffusive E = 0.45t (red squares). Lines are guides to the eye. The averages are computed over 103 independent samples.
